requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":110,"created_at":"2024-06-02T20:03:25Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T11:28:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54653","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR creates a simple `Base.Fix` struct as a general version of `Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` for creating functions with either an argument or keyword argument fixed.

With this change, the entire implementation of these is simply

```julia
const Fix1{F,T} = Fix{1,F,T}
const Fix2{F,T} = Fix{2,F,T}
```

Along with the PR I also add a larger suite of unittests for all three of these functions to complement the existing tests for `Fix1`/`Fix2`.

### Context

There are multiple motivations for this generalization:


1. **Unification**: `Fix1` and `Fix2` into a single, flexible type `Fix`.
   - Simpler interface with fewer sharp edges. There is no preferential treatment of 2-argument functions, and neither is there fixed behavior that `Fix(1|2)` have exclusively 2 arguments.
   - _Principle of Least Astonishment_: One would expect `Fix1`, `Fix2`, etc., to continue in a sequence. Now, one can use `Fix{1}`, `Fix{2}`, `Fix{3}`, and so on, and `Fix{:kw}` for a keyword `kw`. `Fix1` and `Fix2` are there for backwards compatibility but no longer necessary.
   - This also does not constrain future generalisations of `Fix{n}` for multiple arguments. `Fix{1,F,T}` is the obvious generalisation of `Fix1{F,T}`, so this isn't making any major new syntax choices. But in a future PR you could have, e.g., `Fix{(n1,n2)}` for multiple arguments, and it would still be backwards-compatible with this.
3. **Keyword Arguments**: One can now bind a keyword to `Fix`-ed functions
4. **Additional Arguments**: Now, the result of `Fix1`, `Fix2`, and `Fix{n}` propagates an arbitrary number of input arguments and keyword arguments, rather than exclusively handling binary functions.
6. Having an enhanced `Fix` promotes:
    - **Less Compilation**: Since it does not result in new anonymous functions, using `Fix` reduces the need for compilation of commonly-used anonymous functions.
    - **Type Stability**: `Fix`, like `Fix1` and `Fix2`, captures variables in a struct, encouraging users to use a functional paradigm for closures, preventing the type instability from modifying variables within an anonymous function.
    - **Easier Functional Programming**: Allow more complex functional programming by supporting partial functions with _any number of arguments_, beyond the limitations of `Fix1` and `Fix2` which was historically only 2-argument functions.

I also refactor `Fix1` and `Fix2` to be equal to `Fix{1}` and `Fix{2}` respectively. This is backwards compatible.

### Details

`Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` are nice shorthands for creating simple anonymous functions without compiling new functions. They are very common throughout the Julia ecosystem as a shorthand for filling arguments:

- Fix1 https://github.com/search?q=Base.Fix1+language%3Ajulia&type=code
- Fix2 https://github.com/search?q=Base.Fix2+language%3Ajulia&type=code

However, as the names suggest, they can only inject arguments at the first and second index. Furthermore, they are also constrained to work on 2-argument functions exclusively. It seems at various points there had been interest in extending this (see links below) but nobody had gotten around to it so far, so I thought I would take a stab at it as a short project.

This implementation of `Base.Fix` generalises the form as follows:

> `Fix{n}(f, x)`
> `Fix{kw}(f, x)`
> `Fix(f; [kw]=x)`

> A type representing a partially-applied version of a function `f`, with the argument
> "x" fixed at argument `n::Int` or keyword `kw::Symbol`.
> In other words, `Fix{3}(f, x)` behaves similarly to
> `(y1, y2, y3) -> f(y1, y2, x, y3)` for the 4-argument function `f`.

> You may also use this to fix keyword arguments. For example, `Fix(g; a=2)` behaves
> similarly to `x -> g(x; a=2)` for a function `g` with one argument and one keyword argument.
> You can also write this as `Fix{:a}(g, 2)`.

With this more general type, I also rewrite `Fix1` and `Fix2` in this PR.

```julia
const Fix1{F,T} = Fix{1,F,T}
const Fix2{F,T} = Fix{2,F,T}
```

With `Fix{n}`, the code which is executed is roughly as follows:

```julia
function (f::Fix{N})(args::Vararg{Any,M}; kws...) where {N,M}
    if N isa Integer
        if N > 1
            return f.f(args[begin:begin+(N-2)]..., f.x, args[begin+(N-1):end]...; kws...)
        else # N == 1
            return f.f(f.x, args...; kws...)
        end
    else # N isa Symbol
        f_kws = NamedTuple{(N,)}((f.x,))
        return f.f(args...; f_kws..., kws...)
    end
end
```

This means that the captured variable `x` is inserted at the `N`-th position. Keywords are also captured and inserted at the end.

I have added several unittests of this, a docstring, as well as type stability checks, for which it seems to succeed.

I also run the new `Fix1` and `Fix2` test suites added by this PR on the `Fix` version of each struct.

### Examples

**Simple examples:**

To fix the argument `f` with an anonymous function:

```julia
with_f = (a, b, c, d, e, g, h, i, j, k) -> my_func(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k)
```

whereas now it becomes:

```julia
with_f = Base.Fix{6}(my_func, f)
```

I have some usecases like this in SymbolicRegression.jl. I want to fix a single option in a function, and then repeatedly call that function throughout some loop with different input. `Fix1` and `Fix2` are not general enough for this as they only allow 2-argument functions.

A more common use-case I have is to set `MIME"text/plain"` in `print` for tests, which can now be done as `Fix` is no longer limited to 2 arguments:

```julia
s = sprint(Fix{2}(print, MIME"text/plain"()), my_object)
```

without needing to re-compile at each instance.


Also, oftentimes it will also be a keyword that I want to fix. Like, for example,

```julia
sum1 = Fix(sum; dims=1)
sum1(x)
sum1(y) == sum1(z)
```

just nice to have an easy shorthand like in other languages for fixing args. Avoiding anonymous functions means less compilation.

Note this is equivalent to the expression `Fix{:dims}(sum, 1)`.

**<details><summary>In a reduction:</summary>**

Fix1 and Fix2 are useful for short lambda functions like

```julia
sum(Base.Fix1(*, 2), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

to reduce compilation and often improve type stability.

With this new change, you aren't limited to only 2-arg functions, so you can use things like fma in this context:

```julia
sum(Base.Fix{2}(Base.Fix{3}(fma, 2.0), 0.5), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

where this will evaluate as x -> affine(x, 0.5, 2.0).

Another example is a mapreduce, where you would typically want to fix the map and reduction in applying:

```julia
sum(
    Base.Fix{1}(Base.Fix{1}(mapreduce, abs), *),
    [[1, -1], [2, -3, 4], [5]]
)
```

</details>

**<details><summary>In data processing pipelines:</summary>**

Fix can be used to set any number of arguments, and of course also be chained together repeatedly as there is no restriction on 2-arg functions. It makes functional programming easier than with only Fix1 and Fix2.

For example, in a processing pipeline:

```julia
using CSV, DataFrames

affine(a, b, c) = a .+ b .* c

affine_transform_df(path) = (
    CSV.read(path, DataFrame)
        |> dropmissing
        |> Fix{1}(filter, :id => ==(7)) # Use like Fix2
        |> Fix{2}(Fix{3}(affine, 2.0), 0.5)    # Multiple args
        |> Fix{2}(getproperty, :a)
)

affine_transform_df("data.csv")
```

</details>

**<details><summary>For dispatching on keyword-fixed functions:</summary>**

Say that I would like to dispatch on
`sum` for some type, if `dims` is set to an integer. You can do this as follows:

```julia
struct MyType
    x::Float64
end

function (f::Base.Fix{:dims,typeof(sum),Int64})(ar::AbstractArray{MyType})
    return sum(ar; dims=f.k.dims)
end
```

which would result in any use of `Fix(sum; dims=1)` operating on
`Vector{MyType}` to call this special function.

</details>

**<details><summary>Real-world examples**

I would like to use this in my code. Here are some examples:

</summary>



- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/SymbolicRegression.jl/blob/ea03242d099aa189cad3612291bcaf676d77451c/src/InterfaceDynamicExpressions.jl#L177-L191
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DataDrivenDiffEq.jl/blob/ba70d94dd851d5880fa670d6325296512b7435b3/src/solve/koopman.jl#L140
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/pysr_paper/blob/b30687433fb32d3b9784fbedb1480d947dc46fc0/animations/optimization_example.jl#L33
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L212
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L222
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L233
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/UniAdminTools.jl/blob/0f523364fc0f43446ff7c31f6ae3acf6c1ea927f/src/mergescore.jl#L289-L292
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DataDrivenDiffEq.jl/blob/ba70d94dd851d5880fa670d6325296512b7435b3/docs/examples/5_michaelis_menten.jl#L30
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/test/llvm_ir_tests.jl#L8

For example,

```julia
function michaelis_menten(X::AbstractMatrix, p, t::AbstractVector)
    reduce(hcat, map((x,ti)->michaelis_menten(x, p, ti), eachcol(X), t))
end
```

which could now be done with `map(Fix{2}(michaelis_menten, p), eachcol(X), t)`, reducing compilation costs, and avoiding any potential issues with capturing variables in the closure.

Another one would be this:

```julia
    candidate_info_data = DataFrame((
        name = string.(candidates),
        score = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores.mean),
        uncertainty = (x -> round(x, digits = 2)).(summary_scores.std),
        q25 = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores_q[!, "25.0%"]),
        q75 = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores_q[!, "75.0%"]),
    ))
```

with this you could write `Fix(round; digits=3)` and not need to re-compile an anonymous function for each new outer method.

</details>



### Features in other languages

Here are some of the most related features in other languages (all that I could find; there's probably more)

#### Groovy's `.ncurry`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Apache Groovy there is the `<function>.ncurry(index, args...)` to insert arguments at a given index.  This syntax is semantically identical to `Base.Fix`.

From the [documentation](https://web.archive.org/web/20240522202230/https://groovy-lang.org/closures.html#_index_based_currying)
> In case a closure accepts more than 2 parameters, it is possible to set an arbitrary parameter using ncurry:
>
> ```groovy
> def volume = { double l, double w, double h -> l*w*h }
> def fixedWidthVolume = volume.ncurry(1, 2d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthVolume(3d, 4d)
> def fixedWidthAndHeight = volume.ncurry(1, 2d, 4d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthAndHeight(3d)
> ```
>
> 1. the `volume` function defines 3 parameters
> 2. `ncurry` will set the second parameter (index = 1) to 2d, creating a new volume function which accepts length and height
> 3. that function is equivalent to calling `volume` omitting the width
> 4. it is also possible to set multiple parameters, starting from the specified index
> 5. the resulting function accepts as many parameters as the initial one minus the number of parameters set by `ncurry`

</details>

#### Python's `functools.partial`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Python, there is no differentiating between args and kwargs – every function can be passed kwargs.  Therefore, `functools.partial` is semantically similar to `Fix`:

```python
def f(a, b, c, d):
    return a + b * c - d

f_with_b = functools.partial(f, b=2.0)

f_with_b(1.0, d=3.0)
```

which would be equivalent to `Fix{2}(f, 2.0)`.

</details>

#### C++'s `std::bind`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In modern C++, one can use `std::bind` to insert [placeholders](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/utility/functional/bind). This is semantically closer to an anonymous function in Julia, though it *binds* the arguments in a way similar to `Fix1` and `Fix2` do:

```cpp
void f(int n1, int n2, int n3, const int& n4, int n5);
 
int main() {
    using namespace std::placeholders

    auto f2 = std::bind(f, _3, std::bind(g, _3), _3, 4, 5);
    f2(10, 11, 12)  // f(12, g(12), 12, 4, 5)
}
```

</details>

The C++ approach was also briefly mentioned on discourse [back in 2018](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fix1-analogue-of-base-fix2/10161/12).

---

Closes:

- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50553
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/36181

Related issues:

- Initial discussion on Fix1 on [discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fix1-analogue-of-base-fix2/10161/9) which @tpapp added in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/26708
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/15276

Other approaches:

- [FixArgs.jl](https://github.com/goretkin/FixArgs.jl) which stemmed out of https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/36181
  - Note that this package takes a **much** different and more extensive approach to this problem using macros (see [docs](https://goretkin.github.io/FixArgs.jl/dev/#Symbolic-computation-and-lazy-evaluation)), so is likely not in-scope for merging to `Base`. I have written the `Base.Fix` in this PR from scratch based on the same patterns as `Fix1` and `Fix2` but with varargs.
- [AccessorsExtra.jl](https://github.com/JuliaAPlavin/AccessorsExtra.jl)
  - The `FixArgs` implementation in this package is much more closely related to this PR. It works in a similar way although though stores the full signature with a `Placeholder()` set to the replaced arg.
- [FastBroadcast.jl](https://github.com/YingboMa/FastBroadcast.jl) defines an identical (aside from keywords) struct [here](https://github.com/YingboMa/FastBroadcast.jl/blob/9077379705b3d7d1677188c1e18d841daccdbe18/src/FastBroadcast.jl#L14-L18) for internal use

Semi-related issues:

- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/554
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/5571
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/36093 
---

- **Edit 1**: ~~Made `Fix` work for `Vararg` so that you can insert multiple arguments at the specified index.~~
- **Edit 2**: Added keyword~~s~~ to `Fix`.
- **Edit 3**: Switched from `Fix(f, Val(1), arg)` syntax to `Fix{1}(f, arg)`.
- **Edit 4**: After triage, added back the keyword argument~~s~~, and rewrote `Fix1` and `Fix2` in terms of `Fix`.
- **Edit 5**: Added some validation checks for repeated keywords and non-`Int64` `n`
- **Edit 6**: Restricted the number of keyword arguments OR arguments to 1, and made struct more minimal.
- **Edit 7**: After second triage, various cleanup and simplification of code

I mean, if you don't want to (or can't imagine anyone else wanting to) actually use this functionality then I can see why you don't think it important or useful. If you've never used currying or seen a language where it gets used extensively (like the ML family, famously Haskell) then it might not seem particularly "core". The fact that this is a well known exported type that others (writing packages) can use with multiple dispatch might seem unimportant if you've not done much metaprogramming via multiple dispatch. For a concrete example off the top of my head, I could probably use `Fix{3}` or `Fix{4}` with `group` and `innerjoin` in `SplitApplyCombine` in methods extended by `TypedTables` and `AcceleratedArrays` (and this is where I haven't thought too deeply about _this_ particular implemenation).

Looking broadly, what _should_ the most basic, core standard library have? In my opinion, definitely these:

 * the primative CPU types (Int64, Float64, basic arrays, etc)
 * enough stuff to get the FFI working well
 * strings!
 * types/reflection
 * things supported directly by julia syntax (iterators, generators, broadcasting)
 * things supported directly by the julia runtime (concurrency, channels, GC, compilation/interpretation)
 * the manipulation of functions (composition (`\circ`), currying (`Fix`), piping (`|>`), etc)

From my perspective, there are plenty of lower level fruit to move out of `Base` than `Fix1` and `Fix2`. The last category above is _important_!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2236268139/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":112,"created_at":"2024-06-02T20:03:25Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T11:39:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54653","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54653.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR creates a simple `Base.Fix` struct as a general version of `Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` for creating functions with either an argument or keyword argument fixed.

With this change, the entire implementation of these is simply

```julia
const Fix1{F,T} = Fix{1,F,T}
const Fix2{F,T} = Fix{2,F,T}
```

Along with the PR I also add a larger suite of unittests for all three of these functions to complement the existing tests for `Fix1`/`Fix2`.

### Context

There are multiple motivations for this generalization:


1. **Unification**: `Fix1` and `Fix2` into a single, flexible type `Fix`.
   - Simpler interface with fewer sharp edges. There is no preferential treatment of 2-argument functions, and neither is there fixed behavior that `Fix(1|2)` have exclusively 2 arguments.
   - _Principle of Least Astonishment_: One would expect `Fix1`, `Fix2`, etc., to continue in a sequence. Now, one can use `Fix{1}`, `Fix{2}`, `Fix{3}`, and so on, and `Fix{:kw}` for a keyword `kw`. `Fix1` and `Fix2` are there for backwards compatibility but no longer necessary.
   - This also does not constrain future generalisations of `Fix{n}` for multiple arguments. `Fix{1,F,T}` is the obvious generalisation of `Fix1{F,T}`, so this isn't making any major new syntax choices. But in a future PR you could have, e.g., `Fix{(n1,n2)}` for multiple arguments, and it would still be backwards-compatible with this.
3. **Keyword Arguments**: One can now bind a keyword to `Fix`-ed functions
4. **Additional Arguments**: Now, the result of `Fix1`, `Fix2`, and `Fix{n}` propagates an arbitrary number of input arguments and keyword arguments, rather than exclusively handling binary functions.
6. Having an enhanced `Fix` promotes:
    - **Less Compilation**: Since it does not result in new anonymous functions, using `Fix` reduces the need for compilation of commonly-used anonymous functions.
    - **Type Stability**: `Fix`, like `Fix1` and `Fix2`, captures variables in a struct, encouraging users to use a functional paradigm for closures, preventing the type instability from modifying variables within an anonymous function.
    - **Easier Functional Programming**: Allow more complex functional programming by supporting partial functions with _any number of arguments_, beyond the limitations of `Fix1` and `Fix2` which was historically only 2-argument functions.

I also refactor `Fix1` and `Fix2` to be equal to `Fix{1}` and `Fix{2}` respectively. This is backwards compatible.

### Details

`Base.Fix1` and `Base.Fix2` are nice shorthands for creating simple anonymous functions without compiling new functions. They are very common throughout the Julia ecosystem as a shorthand for filling arguments:

- Fix1 https://github.com/search?q=Base.Fix1+language%3Ajulia&type=code
- Fix2 https://github.com/search?q=Base.Fix2+language%3Ajulia&type=code

However, as the names suggest, they can only inject arguments at the first and second index. Furthermore, they are also constrained to work on 2-argument functions exclusively. It seems at various points there had been interest in extending this (see links below) but nobody had gotten around to it so far, so I thought I would take a stab at it as a short project.

This implementation of `Base.Fix` generalises the form as follows:

> `Fix{n}(f, x)`
> `Fix{kw}(f, x)`
> `Fix(f; [kw]=x)`

> A type representing a partially-applied version of a function `f`, with the argument
> "x" fixed at argument `n::Int` or keyword `kw::Symbol`.
> In other words, `Fix{3}(f, x)` behaves similarly to
> `(y1, y2, y3) -> f(y1, y2, x, y3)` for the 4-argument function `f`.

> You may also use this to fix keyword arguments. For example, `Fix(g; a=2)` behaves
> similarly to `x -> g(x; a=2)` for a function `g` with one argument and one keyword argument.
> You can also write this as `Fix{:a}(g, 2)`.

With this more general type, I also rewrite `Fix1` and `Fix2` in this PR.

```julia
const Fix1{F,T} = Fix{1,F,T}
const Fix2{F,T} = Fix{2,F,T}
```

With `Fix{n}`, the code which is executed is roughly as follows:

```julia
function (f::Fix{N})(args::Vararg{Any,M}; kws...) where {N,M}
    if N isa Integer
        if N > 1
            return f.f(args[begin:begin+(N-2)]..., f.x, args[begin+(N-1):end]...; kws...)
        else # N == 1
            return f.f(f.x, args...; kws...)
        end
    else # N isa Symbol
        f_kws = NamedTuple{(N,)}((f.x,))
        return f.f(args...; f_kws..., kws...)
    end
end
```

This means that the captured variable `x` is inserted at the `N`-th position. Keywords are also captured and inserted at the end.

I have added several unittests of this, a docstring, as well as type stability checks, for which it seems to succeed.

I also run the new `Fix1` and `Fix2` test suites added by this PR on the `Fix` version of each struct.

### Examples

**Simple examples:**

To fix the argument `f` with an anonymous function:

```julia
with_f = (a, b, c, d, e, g, h, i, j, k) -> my_func(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k)
```

whereas now it becomes:

```julia
with_f = Base.Fix{6}(my_func, f)
```

I have some usecases like this in SymbolicRegression.jl. I want to fix a single option in a function, and then repeatedly call that function throughout some loop with different input. `Fix1` and `Fix2` are not general enough for this as they only allow 2-argument functions.

A more common use-case I have is to set `MIME"text/plain"` in `print` for tests, which can now be done as `Fix` is no longer limited to 2 arguments:

```julia
s = sprint(Fix{2}(print, MIME"text/plain"()), my_object)
```

without needing to re-compile at each instance.


Also, oftentimes it will also be a keyword that I want to fix. Like, for example,

```julia
sum1 = Fix(sum; dims=1)
sum1(x)
sum1(y) == sum1(z)
```

just nice to have an easy shorthand like in other languages for fixing args. Avoiding anonymous functions means less compilation.

Note this is equivalent to the expression `Fix{:dims}(sum, 1)`.

**<details><summary>In a reduction:</summary>**

Fix1 and Fix2 are useful for short lambda functions like

```julia
sum(Base.Fix1(*, 2), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

to reduce compilation and often improve type stability.

With this new change, you aren't limited to only 2-arg functions, so you can use things like fma in this context:

```julia
sum(Base.Fix{2}(Base.Fix{3}(fma, 2.0), 0.5), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
```

where this will evaluate as x -> affine(x, 0.5, 2.0).

Another example is a mapreduce, where you would typically want to fix the map and reduction in applying:

```julia
sum(
    Base.Fix{1}(Base.Fix{1}(mapreduce, abs), *),
    [[1, -1], [2, -3, 4], [5]]
)
```

</details>

**<details><summary>In data processing pipelines:</summary>**

Fix can be used to set any number of arguments, and of course also be chained together repeatedly as there is no restriction on 2-arg functions. It makes functional programming easier than with only Fix1 and Fix2.

For example, in a processing pipeline:

```julia
using CSV, DataFrames

affine(a, b, c) = a .+ b .* c

affine_transform_df(path) = (
    CSV.read(path, DataFrame)
        |> dropmissing
        |> Fix{1}(filter, :id => ==(7)) # Use like Fix2
        |> Fix{2}(Fix{3}(affine, 2.0), 0.5)    # Multiple args
        |> Fix{2}(getproperty, :a)
)

affine_transform_df("data.csv")
```

</details>

**<details><summary>For dispatching on keyword-fixed functions:</summary>**

Say that I would like to dispatch on
`sum` for some type, if `dims` is set to an integer. You can do this as follows:

```julia
struct MyType
    x::Float64
end

function (f::Base.Fix{:dims,typeof(sum),Int64})(ar::AbstractArray{MyType})
    return sum(ar; dims=f.k.dims)
end
```

which would result in any use of `Fix(sum; dims=1)` operating on
`Vector{MyType}` to call this special function.

</details>

**<details><summary>Real-world examples**

I would like to use this in my code. Here are some examples:

</summary>



- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/SymbolicRegression.jl/blob/ea03242d099aa189cad3612291bcaf676d77451c/src/InterfaceDynamicExpressions.jl#L177-L191
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DataDrivenDiffEq.jl/blob/ba70d94dd851d5880fa670d6325296512b7435b3/src/solve/koopman.jl#L140
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/pysr_paper/blob/b30687433fb32d3b9784fbedb1480d947dc46fc0/animations/optimization_example.jl#L33
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L212
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L222
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/src/stabilization.jl#L233
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/UniAdminTools.jl/blob/0f523364fc0f43446ff7c31f6ae3acf6c1ea927f/src/mergescore.jl#L289-L292
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DataDrivenDiffEq.jl/blob/ba70d94dd851d5880fa670d6325296512b7435b3/docs/examples/5_michaelis_menten.jl#L30
- https://github.com/MilesCranmer/DispatchDoctor.jl/blob/02b46f6060c84c632dedf5e602dd3ca6a2c72d95/test/llvm_ir_tests.jl#L8

For example,

```julia
function michaelis_menten(X::AbstractMatrix, p, t::AbstractVector)
    reduce(hcat, map((x,ti)->michaelis_menten(x, p, ti), eachcol(X), t))
end
```

which could now be done with `map(Fix{2}(michaelis_menten, p), eachcol(X), t)`, reducing compilation costs, and avoiding any potential issues with capturing variables in the closure.

Another one would be this:

```julia
    candidate_info_data = DataFrame((
        name = string.(candidates),
        score = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores.mean),
        uncertainty = (x -> round(x, digits = 2)).(summary_scores.std),
        q25 = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores_q[!, "25.0%"]),
        q75 = (x -> round(x, digits = 3)).(summary_scores_q[!, "75.0%"]),
    ))
```

with this you could write `Fix(round; digits=3)` and not need to re-compile an anonymous function for each new outer method.

</details>



### Features in other languages

Here are some of the most related features in other languages (all that I could find; there's probably more)

#### Groovy's `.ncurry`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Apache Groovy there is the `<function>.ncurry(index, args...)` to insert arguments at a given index.  This syntax is semantically identical to `Base.Fix`.

From the [documentation](https://web.archive.org/web/20240522202230/https://groovy-lang.org/closures.html#_index_based_currying)
> In case a closure accepts more than 2 parameters, it is possible to set an arbitrary parameter using ncurry:
>
> ```groovy
> def volume = { double l, double w, double h -> l*w*h }
> def fixedWidthVolume = volume.ncurry(1, 2d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthVolume(3d, 4d)
> def fixedWidthAndHeight = volume.ncurry(1, 2d, 4d)
> assert volume(3d, 2d, 4d) == fixedWidthAndHeight(3d)
> ```
>
> 1. the `volume` function defines 3 parameters
> 2. `ncurry` will set the second parameter (index = 1) to 2d, creating a new volume function which accepts length and height
> 3. that function is equivalent to calling `volume` omitting the width
> 4. it is also possible to set multiple parameters, starting from the specified index
> 5. the resulting function accepts as many parameters as the initial one minus the number of parameters set by `ncurry`

</details>

#### Python's `functools.partial`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In Python, there is no differentiating between args and kwargs – every function can be passed kwargs.  Therefore, `functools.partial` is semantically similar to `Fix`:

```python
def f(a, b, c, d):
    return a + b * c - d

f_with_b = functools.partial(f, b=2.0)

f_with_b(1.0, d=3.0)
```

which would be equivalent to `Fix{2}(f, 2.0)`.

</details>

#### C++'s `std::bind`

<details><summary>(Expand)</summary>

In modern C++, one can use `std::bind` to insert [placeholders](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/utility/functional/bind). This is semantically closer to an anonymous function in Julia, though it *binds* the arguments in a way similar to `Fix1` and `Fix2` do:

```cpp
void f(int n1, int n2, int n3, const int& n4, int n5);
 
int main() {
    using namespace std::placeholders

    auto f2 = std::bind(f, _3, std::bind(g, _3), _3, 4, 5);
    f2(10, 11, 12)  // f(12, g(12), 12, 4, 5)
}
```

</details>

The C++ approach was also briefly mentioned on discourse [back in 2018](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fix1-analogue-of-base-fix2/10161/12).

---

Closes:

- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50553
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/36181

Related issues:

- Initial discussion on Fix1 on [discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fix1-analogue-of-base-fix2/10161/9) which @tpapp added in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/26708
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/15276

Other approaches:

- [FixArgs.jl](https://github.com/goretkin/FixArgs.jl) which stemmed out of https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/36181
  - Note that this package takes a **much** different and more extensive approach to this problem using macros (see [docs](https://goretkin.github.io/FixArgs.jl/dev/#Symbolic-computation-and-lazy-evaluation)), so is likely not in-scope for merging to `Base`. I have written the `Base.Fix` in this PR from scratch based on the same patterns as `Fix1` and `Fix2` but with varargs.
- [AccessorsExtra.jl](https://github.com/JuliaAPlavin/AccessorsExtra.jl)
  - The `FixArgs` implementation in this package is much more closely related to this PR. It works in a similar way although though stores the full signature with a `Placeholder()` set to the replaced arg.
- [FastBroadcast.jl](https://github.com/YingboMa/FastBroadcast.jl) defines an identical (aside from keywords) struct [here](https://github.com/YingboMa/FastBroadcast.jl/blob/9077379705b3d7d1677188c1e18d841daccdbe18/src/FastBroadcast.jl#L14-L18) for internal use

However, `get!` is optimized via tokens - it will generate a token with `gettoken!`, call `f`, and then call `settokenvalue!` with the token from the first step. This is pretty much the _entire_ premise behind Dictionaries.jl (plus some exploration of what happens if we iterate values not pairs by default).

If `f` mutates the keys of a `Dictionaries.Dictionary` for example, then the token will be invalid and all sorts of badness can occur (we even use `@inbounds` so this is real undefined behavior territory).

This could be patched up by using and checking age counters; these were stripped for a few % of performance since I figured they were only there for `WeakKeyDict` (and concurrent but not parallel mutation in general I - `unique` returns a regular `Vector`:
```julia
julia> sa = StructArray(x=1:10)
10-element StructArray(::UnitRange{Int64}) with eltype NamedTuple{(:x,), Tuple{Int64}}:
 (x = 1,)
 (x = 2,)
...

julia> unique(sa)
10-element Vector{NamedTuple{(:x,), Tuple{Int64}}}:
 (x = 1,)
 (x = 2,)
...
```
After - returns array of the same type:

```julia
julia> unique(sa)
10-element StructArray(::Vector{Int64}) with eltype NamedTuple{(:x,), Tuple{Int64}}:
 (x = 1,)
 (x = 2,)
...
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47970/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47970/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1920210830","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47970#issuecomment-1920210830","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47970","id":1920210830,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ydBeO","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-01T00:00:01Z","updated_at":"2024-02-01T00:00:01Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"FWIW the library code I have written has always assumed `unique` should return a `similar` array based on how it works in `Base` on ranges. been running some package tests on the upcoming julia version (1.10rc) and noticed this error newly introduced there, compared to 1.9:
```julia
julia> Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.9:
Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.10:
ERROR: Tuple field type cannot be Union{}
```
Not sure if introduced deliberately or as a side-effect of some other change, but it breaks perfectly working code. And I don't see it anywhere in `Tuple` docs that it shouldn't support all element types.

The above is an MWE, and below is the simplified situation where I actually encountered it:
```julia
julia> X = StructArray(a=Union{}[], b=[]);
# 1.10 throws error right here
# 1.9 lets you proceed just fine

# can work with the non-Union{} column:
julia> X.b
Any[]

# can retrieve the Union{} column as well, of course cannot access its elements
julia> X.a |> typeof
Vector{Union{}} (alias for Array{Union{}, 1})
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1928869530","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385#issuecomment-1928869530","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385","id":1928869530,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5y-Daa","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-06T06:32:42Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T06:32:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@vtjnash thank you, that is quite helpful.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1928869530/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

version"},{"id":688843543,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2ODg4NDM1NDM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/triage","name":"triage","color":"e4003c","default":false,"description":"This call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":87,"created_at":"2023-12-04T01:52:52Z","updated_at":"2024-02-29T00:09:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I've been running some package tests on the upcoming julia version (1.10rc) and noticed this error newly introduced there, compared to 1.9:
```julia
julia> Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.9:
Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.10:
ERROR: Tuple field type cannot be Union{}
```
Not sure if introduced deliberately or as a side-effect of some other change, but it breaks perfectly working code. And I don't see it anywhere in `Tuple` docs that it shouldn't support all element types.

The above is an MWE, and below is the simplified situation where I actually encountered it:
```julia
julia> X = StructArray(a=Union{}[], b=[]);
# 1.10 throws error right here
# 1.9 lets you proceed just fine

# can work with the non-Union{} column:
julia> X.b
Any[]

# can retrieve the Union{} column as well, of course cannot access its elements
julia> X.a |> typeof
Vector{Union{}} (alias for Array{Union{}, 1})
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1970142426","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385#issuecomment-1970142426","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385","id":1970142426,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM51bfza","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-29T00:09:13Z","updated_at":"2024-02-29T00:09:13Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks @mbauman for getting into the details here - very helpful!

For my part, I'm 100% OK with a bugfix being technically breaking (all bugfixes can break something). And I'm really not bothered if `Tuple{Union{}}` isn't a "type that exists as I wrote it", but it did seem strange to create a user-facing error at this point when `apply_type` (or whatever we call it - "datatype instantiation" seems to be the terminology in the C code) returning `Union{}` is consistent and bug-free.

So I also support moving forward with #53452 and #53516.

> `Tuple{Int}` and `Tuple{Nothing}` are both concrete types and are not equal to each other so their intersection must be empty.

Right. Yet because of covariance, the type `Tuple{Union{}}` would be a subtype of both of these _if_ such a type exists.

> failed to notice that `StructArray{Tuple{Union{}}}` and `StructArray{Union{}}` are type-equal?

And just like the above, this seem to be resolved by making `Tuple{Union{}} === Union{}`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1970142426/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35293","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35293/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35293/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35293/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35293","id":589698245,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1ODk2OTgyNDU=","number":35293,"title":"Deprecate/remove map(f) and foreach(f)","user":{"login":"tkf","id":29282,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5Mjgy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/29282?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf","html_url":"https://github.com/tkf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tkf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":232418,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMzI0MTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/help%20wanted","name":"help wanted","color":"0b02e1","default":true,"description":"Indicates that a maintainer wants help on an issue or pull request"},{"id":1363386,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzYzMzg2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/breaking","name":"breaking","color":"800000","default":false,"description":"This change will break code"},{"id":522769435,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw1MjI3Njk0MzU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/collections","name":"collections","color":"09aeb1","default":false,"description":"Data structures holding multiple items, e.g. sets"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":17,"created_at":"2020-03-29T01:59:03Z","updated_at":"2024-01-16T20:55:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It might be useful to define curried version of iterator transforms such as `Iterators.map(f) = x -> Iterators.map(f, x)` (after #34352) and `Iterators.filter(f) = x -> Iterators.filter(f, x)` (ref #33027, https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-605384271).  However, since the eager counter part `map(f)` is currently defined as `f()`, it may confuse users when the difference between `map(f)` and `Iterators.map(f)` are more than laziness.

Can we remove `map(f)` at some point? (I think whether or not it is desirable to do this within Julia 1.x is up to core devs.)  I think it makes sense to also remove `foreach(f)` when removing `map(f)` for consistency.

`map(f)` was added by @stevengj in #17318. The reasoning was that it is better to be consistent with `f.()`. I agree that considering `f.()` as a special case of `f.(args...)` makes sense for broadcasting; i.e., the output dimension of broadcasting is `reduce(max, ndims_of_arguments)` when `ndims_of_arguments` is not empty.  Since `ndims` is always a non-negative integer, it is reasonable to use the identity element of `max` on non-negative integers which is `0`, when there is no argument.  However, this argument relies on that the "`ndims`" of the output is flexibly determined (i.e., using the largest `ndims`) in broadcasting.  As this is not the case in `map`, I think it makes less sense to define `map(f)` as `f()`.  OTOH, I think curried `map` is a useful definition.

`foreach(f)` was added by @JeffBezanson in #13774.  It seems there was no discussion for adding `foreach(f)`.

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":36,"created_at":"2023-12-04T01:52:52Z","updated_at":"2024-01-23T09:51:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I've been running some package tests on the upcoming julia version (1.10rc) and noticed this error newly introduced there, compared to 1.9:
```julia
julia> Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.9:
Tuple{Union{}}
# 1.10:
ERROR: Tuple field type cannot be Union{}
```
Not sure if introduced deliberately or as a side-effect of some other change, but it breaks perfectly working code. And I don't see it anywhere in `Tuple` docs that it shouldn't support all element types.

The above is an MWE, and below is the simplified situation where I actually encountered it:
```julia
julia> X = StructArray(a=Union{}[], b=[]);
# 1.10 throws error right here
# 1.9 lets you proceed just fine

# can work with the non-Union{} column:
julia> X.b
Any[]

# can retrieve the Union{} column as well, of course cannot access its elements
julia> X.a |> typeof
Vector{Union{}} (alias for Array{Union{}, 1})
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1905681656","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385#issuecomment-1905681656","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52385","id":1905681656,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5xlmT4","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-23T09:51:26Z","updated_at":"2024-01-23T09:51:26Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"My suggestion for immutable structures would be to allow the actual type, but disallow its construction (even via tricks like `unsafe_load`, `reinterpret`, and similar). Basically there would be no valid layout for the compiler or runtime to work with.

For mutable "locations" maybe we allow it with the layout of `Union{}` taking zero bytes, yet attempting to get (or set) the value results in a runtime error. AFAICT at the moment sometimes `Union{}` takes zero bytes to represent (in a struct?) and sometimes is pointer-width (in an array?).

I would also vote for allowing `Tuple{Union{}}` in Julia 1.10.1.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1905681656/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

E.g. it might be OK to instantiate an empty `Vector{Union{}}` but not a `Tuple{Union{}}` (which would be similar to trying to instantiate a `Union{}`, which is plainly impossible).

Still - in terms of the implementation, having `Tuple{Union{}}` become `Union{}` seems better for users than a runtime error. Especially for generic code.

As for `Core.Compiler.return_type`, I'm honestly happy to use whatever works. Note that sometimes whatever logic is used in `Base` in methods such as `map(f, ::Vector)` needs to be replicated in packages for other data structures. In Julia 1.10 this ultimately uses `Base.@default_eltype` to infer the eltype (which itself uses `Core.Compiler.return_type`).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1870888144/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

etc."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":19,"created_at":"2023-03-18T11:19:14Z","updated_at":"2023-11-01T08:27:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The documentation for `mapreduce` says that it is unspecified if the `init` key argument is used for non-empty collections. Since there is a single `julia` implementation leaving the behavior unspecified is bad. In general the `init` argument is a starting point that you want to combine with the rest. For example
```julia
sol = nlsolve(f!, initial_x)
 # mapreduce doesn't specify if `init` is used. Need to use reduce(..., map(...))
 if mapreduce(map(v -> v > 0, &,  sol.zero, init=converged(sol))
     return ...
 else
   ...
 end
```
Using the `init` argument to specify the result in case of an empty collection is a special case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1788588646","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042#issuecomment-1788588646","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042","id":1788588646,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5qm7Jm","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-01T08:27:25Z","updated_at":"2023-11-01T08:27:25Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"My memory is a bit vague. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50063","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50063/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50063/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50063/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50063","id":1740785304,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5SJC_g","number":50063,"title":"A more nicely formatted output for the `display()` function with `NamedTuple`s","user":{"login":"tomchor","id":13205162,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzMjA1MTYy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13205162?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor","html_url":"https://github.com/tomchor","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-06-05T02:41:07Z","updated_at":"2023-06-12T12:17:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/50063","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50063","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50063.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50063.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This is an attempt to create an output for `display()` with `NamedTuple`s that's more human-readable.

My first attempt is an output that almost exactly matches that of `Dict`s:

```julia
julia> nt = (a=3, b=5, g=5, d=9,)
NamedTuple with 4 entries:
  a = 3
  b = 5
  g = 5
  d = 9
```

imo that could be good enough, and given that's virtually a copy-paste from `Dict` users should be familiar with it. But I'm hoping to have a discussion about it.

Note that in the output above I'm purposefully omiting the colon (`:` ) from the `NamedTuple`'s keys' symbols to mimic the syntax of how `NamedTuple`s are defined (i.e. we define it as `nt = (a=3, b=5)`, not as `nt = (:a=3, :b=5)`).

One of the reasons for the way it currently is, is that it mimics actual sytax - it is kind of like `repr` (but we don't _need_ to do that for `show` let alone `display`; arrays also have built-in syntax but display this way). One thought experiment I would suggest considering here is could we, or should we, do a similar thing for `Tuple`? I generally like to think of tuples and named tuples as a kind of pair of similar things (modulo variance). What about the default display for structs? If you are exploring at the REPL that could be kind of nice...

One point in favour is one thing I love to do with named tuples is use them in a`Vector{NamedTuple{...}}` as a lightweight table (or dataframe) structure. When you want to inspect a "row" of this table, the output is currently a little... condensed. Let's be honest - it's hard to read. This would definitely fix that. (In fact, I kind of wonder whether displaying the typing information would be helpful in such a context - even though this might be inferrable from the printed values it could pay to be explicit).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1587226931/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004","id":1733145348,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5nTbME","number":50004,"title":"A more nicely formatted output for the `display()` function with `NamedTuple`s","user":{"login":"tomchor","id":13205162,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzMjA1MTYy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13205162?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor","html_url":"https://github.com/tomchor","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tomchor/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-05-30T23:13:06Z","updated_at":"2023-06-12T12:19:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If I define a `Dict` use `display()` on it (or just type it in the REPL) I get a very nice and human-readable display:

```julia
julia> dict = Dict(:a=>4, :b=>5, :c=>6.0, :d=>"7")
Dict{Symbol, Any} with 4 entries:
  :a => 4
  :b => 5
  :d => "7"
  :c => 6.0

julia> display(dict)
Dict{Symbol, Any} with 4 entries:
  :a => 4
  :b => 5
  :d => "7"
  :c => 6.0
```

Beautiful formatting!

However, doing the same for tuples provides a results that's not as readable:

```julia
julia> tup = (a=4, b=5, c=6.0, d="7")
(a = 4, b = 5, c = 6.0, d = "7")

julia> display(tup)
(a = 4, b = 5, c = 6.0, d = "7")

julia> @show tup
tup = (a = 4, b = 5, c = 6.0, d = "7")
(a = 4, b = 5, c = 6.0, d = "7")
```

That's not a big issue for small `NamedTuple`s, but it makes big ones significantly hard to read.

Is there a reason to justify this difference? If not, I'd like to request a visualization for `NamedTuples` (and perhaps `Tuple`s too?) that's more inline with the one we see in `Dict`.

Alternatively, it would be nice if one could at least use the splat operator with `@show` or something to unpack items in the a `NamedTuple` for easier display. It would work something like:

```julia
julia> tup = (a=4, b=5, c=6.0, d="7")
(a = 4, b = 5, c = 6.0, d = "7")

julia> @show tup...
  a = 4
  b = 5
  d = "7"
  c = 6.0
```

Anyway, I'd be happy to contribute once (and if) a proper path forward is agreed upon! (Depending if my Julia skills end up matching the task... :grimacing: )","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1587230593","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004#issuecomment-1587230593","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004","id":1587230593,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5emzeB","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-06-12T12:19:35Z","updated_at":"2023-06-12T12:19:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I mentioned this on the PR but I'll add it here for completeness - would you think a similar thing should happen for `Tuple`s? ```julia

line:col│ tree                                   │ file_name

:("prefix $($(Expr(:format, 1.0, :(digits = 2)))) suffix")

is an attempt to create an output for `display()` with `NamedTuple`s that's more human-readable.

My first attempt is an output that almost exactly matches that of `Dict`s:

```julia
julia> nt = (a=3, b=5, g=5, d=9,)
NamedTuple with 4 entries:
  a = 3
  b = 5
  g = 5
  d = 9
```

imo that could be good enough, and given that's virtually a copy-paste from `Dict` users should be familiar with it. But I'm hoping to have a discussion about it.

Note that in the output above I'm purposefully omiting the colon (`:` ) from the `NamedTuple`'s keys' symbols to mimic the syntax of how `NamedTuple`s are defined (i.e. we define it as `nt = (a=3, b=5)`, not as `nt = (:a=3, :b=5)`).

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50004

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":426,"created_at":"2017-12-08T19:10:10Z","updated_at":"2023-01-11T12:48:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/24990","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

* ~~Only a single underscore is allowed.  `f(_,_)` throws an error: this case seems ambiguous to me (do you want `x -> f(x,x)` or `x,y -> f(x,y)`?), so it seemed better to punt on this for now.  We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

The implementation is pretty trivial.  If people are in favor, I will add
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Fix interaction with broadcasting `f.(x, _)`
- [ ] Fix interaction with keyword arguments `f(x; y=_)`
- [ ] Support chained comparisons, e.g. `3 ≤ _ ≤ 10`, since they parse as a single expression?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/reactions","total_count":276,"+1":162,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":11,"confused":2,"heart":84,"rocket":9,"eyes":4},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1379897807","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-1379897807","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990","id":1379897807,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5SP5HP","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-12T07:06:42Z","updated_at":"2023-01-12T07:06:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> simple, composable, and general solution

If you want that, it seems you need something where the boundaries of the closure are easily understood and _controllable_ by the user (I mean look at the length of this thread - that's the central point of contention). Again _half_-jokingly, if you truly want it general and composable (multiple arguments, arbitrary placement of boundaries, nestable) you may even want something quite like `->`, where we already have `x -> x.property` or `x -> getproperty(obj, x)` or `y -> x === y`.

I thought the point of this discussion is to see if there is something small and _specific_ (less general and capable than `->`). Believe me, I also want some easier and prettier than `Base.Fix2(:property)`, and more introspectable than `x -> x.property`.

> promotes furthering the existing deleterious trend

There's another trend to be aware of - increasing the amount of syntax in Julia or the complexity of "lowering" makes it harder to read code and understand what it means and does. The language comes in layers - there's going to be more resistance to complicating the type system, syntax, lowering, etc than adding another overload to a generic function, and that's a good thing.

That said I think there is space for some simple/composable/generic syntax to help us out. For example "piping" or "method chaining" can be pretty nice and there are some good ideas around for improving that (e.g. https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148, https://github.com/c42f/Underscores.jl, etc). But there's nothing yet I've seen quite as easy and composable/user-controllable as when dot-broadcasting was introduced, for example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1379897807/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

is a regression from 0.6.2 which affects DataFramesMeta (https://github.com/JuliaStats/DataFramesMeta.jl/issues/88). Under some very particular circumstances, expression interpolation reverses the order of keys in a dictionary, compared with the order obtained in other places inside the same function. This is a problem when the function relies on the order of keys matching that of values (as with DataFrames macros).

In the following example, `:a` appears before `:b` in the two first lines inside the quoted block, but that's the contrary for the third line.
```julia
function with_helper()
    membernames = Dict{Symbol,Int}(:a => 1, :b => 2)
    quote
        $(keys(membernames)...)
        $(map(key -> :($key), keys(membernames))...)
        $(map(key -> :(somedict[$key]), keys(membernames))...)
    end
end

julia> with_helper()
quote
    #= REPL[1]:4 =#
    a
    b
    #= REPL[1]:5 =#
    a
    b
    #= REPL[1]:6 =#
    somedict[b]
    somedict[a]
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/26359/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/26359/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1367771923","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/26359#issuecomment-1367771923","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/26359","id":1367771923,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5RhosT","user":{"login":"andyferris","id":2974526,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NzQ1MjY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2974526?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris","html_url":"https://github.com/andyferris","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andyferris/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-30T07:32:23Z","updated_at":"2022-12-30T07:32:23Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Appologies, I realize this issue is closed but it seemed most straightforward to reply to @hraftery here.

I think it's desirable that users easily have access to both of those semantics (mapping sets to sets, and mapping sets to dictionaries).

We now have `Iterators.map` being another shorthand for generators. However `Iterators.map` is just meant to be the _lazy_ version of `map`. Would it be surprising if `for x in map(f, set); ...; end` behaved differently to `for x in Iterators.map(f, set); ... ; end`? I think it could be. Generally you want to use `Iterators.map` as "I want to map the entries of this thing and then iterate over _that_, but I just want to save some memory in the meanwhile". (In my mental model, `map` is a generic function with semantics on generic iterables, and richer yet compatible semantics on richer data structures).

> So I guess what I'm saying is that restoring the equivalent of:
> 
> ```map(f, A::Union{AbstractArray,AbstractSet}) = collect_similar(A, Generator(f,A))```

Note that the meaning of `similar` on sets and dictionaries could be defined so that this is preservered (as is already the case in Dictionaries.jl).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1367771923/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
with_logger(my_logger) do ()
    # my code
end
```

However this crashed because Julia created a one-argument anonymous function and `with_logger` calls the function with zero arguments. I came up with syntax this because when I create an anonymous function with `do` with more than one argument I normally do make sure to use brackets like so:

```julia
f(a, b, c) do (x, y, z)
    # code...
end
```

and since that works for 1 or more arguments (e.g. `(x)` and `(x,)` both work), I would expect the `()` would create a zero argument function. In fact, I am struggling to understand _what_ it thinks is happening with that empty tuple... e.g. does it create an argument named `Symbol("()")`?

---

PS - I understand that

```julia
with_logger(my_logger) do
    # my code
end
```
